summary: Gruber describes his email management system built around flagging messages to keep his inbox under control, and explains a workaround for the iPhone Mail app's lack of flagging support. He uses a simple two-mailbox system (inbox and archive) where flagged messages stay in the inbox and everything else gets swept to the archive via AppleScript. The iPhone made email triage while traveling much easier, but its inability to flag messages forced him into the bad habit of marking messages as unread. His workaround involves creating a special '[Flag]' mailbox in each IMAP account, moving messages there from the iPhone, then running an AppleScript on his Mac to flag them and move them back to the inbox.
key_points: Using 'unread' status as a to-do marker is a harmful habit that undermines the utility of unread counts
The iPhone revolutionized email triage by enabling reading messages in small windows throughout the day
iPhone Mail's lack of flagging support was a significant gap that forced users into workarounds
A simple system of inbox + archive + flagging can keep email manageable without achieving literal Inbox Zero
AppleScript bridges the gap between iPhone Mail's limitations and desktop Mail's full feature set
money_quotes: One habit I've found to be harmful is using "unread" to mean anything other than "new message".
Breaking myself of this habit significantly increased my email efficiency.
So while I'm not — and to be honest, never am — at inbox zero, I am almost always, at the end of each day, at unread zero.
The primary thing I want to do with email on the iPhone is triage — most messages I can read once and forget.
I really want the number in that badge to represent new messages, not new messages and all the old ones marked unread because you didn't want to deal with them on the iPhone.
Pre-iPhone, I'd often fall woefully behind on email from DF readers while I was traveling. The iPhone makes it easy to read messages here and there, a few minutes at a time, throughout the day.
key_insight: The iPhone's lack of message flagging forced a creative workaround that reveals how small missing features in mobile apps can undermine otherwise effective personal productivity systems.
